speaker: interviewer
text: So you move into your apartment, and what happens then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you before. We needed to-- a [INAUDIBLE] to have a door. And we get back our [? tavern ?] license. But I really don't help too much because I had my profession. And probably people liked how I designed and how I make it. And I went-- neighbor-- not neighbors, people who can afford it. Most of the time not Jewish people. Some of them. Like for instance, Morton cousin was a lawyer and I went to their house to make some dresses for the wife and she helped finishing it. Finishing the dresses, or-- or skirt or suit, I never like it. I-- I felt like my time worth it more than finishing. It wasn't always like machine, you know. And always have somebody. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I get some people from money. You know, a certain amount of money for a day. And-- and when I went out to the farmhouse where it was interesting. We don't-- in the city where we moved to the apartment, we have water, and we have light electric. Our old house, we don't have the electric. We have kerosene and [? beautiful. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyhow. And beside money, I get flour, and sugar, and goose. And I even-- get the separate room up there with the regular bathroom. So it was very nice. I-- they treated me very nicely. And was once a time when these people, friends who lived, let's say 6 kilometer. And they asked if I would like to go there. But I always went home for Shabbos and the next day.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this went on. Morton and the young people was in the labor camp, but some of the boy was in the army. And let's see, the mother died, or father died. Naturally, they came home and all of a sudden, they don't call them back. And they came because the ghetto in Auschwitz. But [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] wasn't too many happy things. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the family you worked for?

speaker: interviewee
text: The mayor name was [? Faher, ?] the last name. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] they call here and the wife. And their daughter's name was Martha. And-- and also this [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE], she [INAUDIBLE] alone. You know, where I went. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's really-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you see, you never know what come handy. Let's see, if I go to the Jewish college-- it was always my dream to be a Kindergarten and first grade teacher. Then what can I do? We cannot-- you know. I couldn't get the job. And this way, unexpectedly, then change the profession. And that's helped here in America. That's helped in the Auschwitz and in the-- many places. What was the situation in the street?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the situation in the street before the German occupation in 1944?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- it wasn't-- I cannot very remember. I honestly tell you, we don't have time to go out too much in the street. When they come the evening-- like for instance, in Tokaj I had long sanatorium. And his wife and the parents was allowed, I think, to sell for them even with yellow star. And when I went to Budapest to get I want to officially came to America. And my younger sister who I thought she went there to help. [? This ?] [? is-- ?] accidentally, I even have that picture. It was nice people. But have some young-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean-- I mean, not really highly educated-- and now is-- is hit the Jews or kill the Jews they always was ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe the German occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe the German occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: In March 19th, if I could remember. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't tell all things. How about just I tell the day after it was over? This was, if I could remember, end of March or something. Morton know. I don't want to bother. This time, the mayor wife came to fitting. And I cannot remember why-- why I wasn't go to their place.

speaker: interviewer
text: And before she came, the drummer came every street corner. And-- and here and here. And he said, the new law is to the Jews cannot leave the premises. And in an hour later, this lady came. Mrs. [? Faher ?] to fitting. And I said to her, what going on? Then-- she said, if something going to happen, I send down the butler. And the butler only going to ask. Because probably she don't trust the butler or something to give the red, white polka dot material.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she don't have the chance to come to send the butler. She left about half an hour later. I look out the window and I see my aunt and-- and neighbors. Jewish people, leaving the street with the [INAUDIBLE] with-- you know, the last minute when they said, you cannot take a suitcase or anything. A sheet and put the necessary things to go. And they came into our house. Two-- two policemen. You know, the gendarme with the feather. And one of them, they know us very well. So they said then, we have to take you to-- to the temple, you know, to the synagogue. But get for [INAUDIBLE] everybody [INAUDIBLE] in this [INAUDIBLE] to some warm thing because going to be a cold night. We don't know at that time then was walking the [INAUDIBLE] from here our temple. Then all the 1,200 people was in the synagogue. Children. But the interesting thing was, I told you, we had a cow. And my middle sister, I never can do that. She said, just wait. I go to milking the cow. And that was in many children's life saved. You know, took the milk. At night, children was crying. Baby was crying. So I don't know how many people drink from this milk. And they closed the door. You know, we-- we don't know we're going to bomb it or we was at the floor. You know, they have not chairs, but the benches like the small synagogue here. And the people have every seat and the youngster was on the floor. And-- and it was terrible. But in the morning, they open it up and needed who was able to to help to make a place where you go out to do your necessary things. Then in our Tokaj temple was a big yard and school rooms. So the-- all the rooms we occupied. In one room maybe five or six family. You know, one corner the young girls, the other corner the [INAUDIBLE]. You know. And at the floor, I don't know, some people bringed some straw. But they had a Serb bakery man. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I forgot his name. He sended every day some bread to us. And also this Martha, the mayor daughter, they bring some bread or something through the fence. And then the police said to them, if you going to come again, we're going to throw you in with the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we was there a couple weeks. And took us to the ghetto. Many people needed to walk the six miles. My father, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who was with the First War, give our cow. And he came with horse and the bigger wagon. And-- and not only us, and the three sister, my parents but whoever was able to fit in the big wagon. We went to the next city and then Satoraljaujhely we had the ghetto. So we was in a ghetto. Maybe interesting to tell. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Next day or when we get to the synagogue, next day or the following day, the mayor wife sent down the butler and told the gendarme, the police, that I should go home with the police to give the material. You know. To-- so the butler came to the house, and the police came with me, and I give the material. And we went-- went out from the apartment to the street with the police. And my neighbor came and asked, Ilonka, would you like to give me something that I should hide it when you come back? I had-- not this one. When my fiance came down, ask my father. I'm not going to wear the diamond ring, then I should wear this one. And when he came back, he gave a new one. I put it off my, and I-- and I said to my neighbor, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. If I don't come back-- because she have same age daughter like me-- then she can have it. But if I come back, I want it back. She give it back when I came back. I give to my sister. And my sister, my daughter when I get married. It was a [? robbery. ?] So anyhow, this policeman, we walked. He said to me, Ilonka, I need to have a cup of coffee. I go to the coffee shop. And you said you would like to buy a folding cups. You know, to-- because we don't have it nothing. So I went to buy this cup, and he was at the coffee shop. After that, I just think about in Auschwitz or when we came back, maybe he meant that I should disappear. You know, he went to the coffee shop. I went to the store. He had many [INAUDIBLE]. How can I disappear, leave my older parents and sister in the temple? So I went to the coffee shop. Tell him we can go back to the temple. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You know, it's-- it's-- I'm not regret, you know. Because I would be so guilty, then I would-- you know. So from the ghetto-- in the ghetto was baby born. People died. There was wedding. And then there came some news that we're going to go other side of Hungary to work. But cannot take more than 3 kilogram. So we pack and [INAUDIBLE]. And things people came where we lived with many other people to cut the blanket to be small to able.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [NON-ENGLISH] was when [? order ?] was. And day before, I went visit my cousin and uncle. And next day, they disappeared already. They went the second transport or-- or went we went the third. I cannot remember. And we needed to go to the cattle wagon. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry-- sorry to interrupt you. How long were you in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: About [INAUDIBLE] how long. From Pesach till [NON-ENGLISH], that's two months, or maybe a month and a half, or two months. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the ghetto separated from the rest of the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was a gate. Not-- not only gate, a fence. People went under the fence, came back. For instance, my first cousin wife. My first cousin was killed but the wife with four children one night disappeared. The husband from Budapest sended down somebody. And middle of the night with two pyas boy. And she went. And they disappeared. They-- they survived in Budapest with the Swedish house or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what food did you get?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto? I cannot even remember, you know. But I remember before we went to Auschwitz, my middle sister-- who I said I helped to sewing this small blanket and things. And people had some money to put money and-- to put away and maybe at the shoulder, at the pocket, or-- or whatever. Then she made it some biscuit. Then maybe they said, too, they is going to be when we go the other side of Hungary. Such a lie.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one thing I still don't know what happened. When we're marching, you know, with my parents, and sister, and people who stayed with us in this house, and I saw my father's older brother who was very religious. And they're hitting him because he don't want to go. Because [INAUDIBLE] not supposed to [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I just never had a chance to ask his daughter-in-law who disappeared-- you know, from the ghetto-- if ever heard heard what happened. If they kill him there or-- or the wagon. Because you couldn't stop. You needed to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Engel [? Shandor. ?] [? Shandor, ?] the first name. And [INAUDIBLE]-- and he was very well known in Tokaj. Before they get us to go to the temple, they get him earlier morning, and Morton, cousin the lawyer. A couple-- how can I say-- wealthy, you know, people they get with us. Think about it. That's just [INAUDIBLE]. You know what this mean. Then they're holding that maybe they want to have money. And do you mention that and I forget to tell you. Then what [INAUDIBLE] or how they said. What-- like we needed to give all the silver and gold to the city hall. In the picture, what I looked myself then a young. In Europe, you do you don't change the earring every day. And I was the fourth girl. And a three blue stone little earring. And there was the jewelry man who helped in the city hall. He almost cried. I couldn't take it out, the earring, because he make it close well. Because I used to, like a little girl, I used to use all the time. So my mother then bought this earring. Maybe I was five-year-old. And that time, I was over 20. I remember, he said, I put this earring through Ilonka's ear. And he almost-- you know, he-- he needed to do it because you know. So you're asking what other humiliation. What happened before things is not important. Not the real things. It's not important, the life.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you live in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a-- somebody house. You know, because they make it the ghetto. Not everybody was Jewish house. So they had such a long street. You know, not only from Tokaj they was in the ghetto. In Satoraljaujhely, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], get us to many places. Many nearby city.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people lived together?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a-- in a room? We was five. We was seven-- was seven. I-- I cannot-- I think four family in one room. One kitchen and was maybe the house, two-bedroom house. Or--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like to live so crowded?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a pleasant here. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] Could you imagine? I hope never happen again. You are at the corner, you know, with your parents, your sister. Next to you, have a man, a boy, or a child, or you know. It is-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know how we can do that. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyhow, we went with the cattle wagon. And they us-- Sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: You want to hear something?

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else that you'd like to tell me about the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother-in-law, youngest brother was very sick and he died. And no [INAUDIBLE], you know, no men [INAUDIBLE]. I remember I went to the-- not joined, but the Jewish. At least one sheet somehow get. It's-- it's-- it was terrible, really. The only things was then we heard the next day, then so and so is getting married. You know, it-- it's-- and then a baby born.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, it's-- it's-- already in the ghetto, we wasn't normal. I don't think so because-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] you know something, I don't remember what meal we ate. Could you believe that? I have to talk to my two other sisters in New York. Because one of them really have wonderful memory. Who guarded they ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who died?

speaker: interviewer
text: Guarded.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who guarded the ghetto? The Hungarian people. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we was in the cattle wagon. A little baby was next to me. These people died. People needed to do their necessary. And I don't know. One time they stopped the wagon and people had some water or something. My sister said when we talked about it. She give something and get some water. So anyhow, we arrived to Auschwitz. Could you describe how you boarded?

speaker: interviewee
text: How we arrived?

speaker: interviewer
text: How you-- how you boarded? How you got on the train, on the cattle car?

speaker: interviewee
text: How we boarded? You know, we went to walking. And the cattle cars, you know, needed to help older people to go in it because it was high. And-- and-- I could see my father in this corner was davening. Because it was [INAUDIBLE]. My mother next to. And I was-- there was a little baby with-- not a carriage, but with something. And cried and tried to make it, too. And my sister and people who I never know. And then my neighbors. Actually, the-- a good friend of us, and old friend who's niece came, and the baby, and his brother-in-law. And you know, people who wasn't in the labor camp. And people who come home, some funeral, and went back maybe a week before or something. Then-- And I cannot remember how long took this. That's the reason after when I talked to you, I called up Martha, my daughter. And I told last night 11:30, Henry. I think before I die I would like to go to Auschwitz, and Stutthof, and Torun, and Magdeburg where I will somehow. Till now, I never want to go. And we arrived there. We don't know it. This is Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe the journey a little more?

speaker: interviewee
text: How can I describe? It was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] crying, yelling, people get crazy. And-- and some people's [INAUDIBLE] is-- is-- cannot describe. Terrible. How could-- how could they do it?

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll change tapes.
